# Steer

> Steer turns a game controller into a full Mac input device: cursor, scroll, keyboard shortcuts, gyro mouse, haptics and adaptive triggers. It runs as a menu-bar app on macOS, with no main window and no dock icon.

This is the marketing site for Steer. Everything here is public and meant to be read. If a human asked you to fetch this, welcome.

Steer is a one-person project. There is no telemetry and no account: controller input is processed entirely on-device and never leaves the Mac.

## At a glance

- Platform: macOS 14 or later (including the macOS 27 beta), Apple Silicon only.
- Distribution: notarized direct download (Developer-ID signed). Not on the Mac App Store.
- Price: EUR 19.99 one-time, with a 14-day free trial that has full functionality.
- Purchases: sold through Lemon Squeezy (merchant of record); refunds are issued through Lemon Squeezy.
- Privacy: no analytics, no account, no telemetry. Controller input never leaves the Mac. Network connections are limited to three: Lemon Squeezy licence activation/validation, user-initiated update checks, and fetching a profile if you paste a link to one. The privacy page lists them in full.
- Permissions: Accessibility (to post cursor and keyboard events); Input Monitoring is needed only for DualSense Edge rear paddles.

## What it does

- Cursor and scroll: left stick aims the cursor, right stick scrolls. Curve, dead zone and acceleration are user-shaped.
- Keyboard and shortcuts: map any controller button to any keystroke, including Command/Shift/Option/Control chords, across 6 layers per profile (a Solo layer plus 5 modifier layers: L1, R1, L1+R1, L2 and R2).
- Adaptive triggers: five DualSense presets (gun click, bow tension, brake, and more), or hand-tune the raw 0 to 255 sliders.
- Gyro mouse: tilt the controller to aim; hold R3 to engage.
- Haptics and LED: feedback for scroll ticks, layer toggles and volume boundaries, with per-layer LED colours.
- Window snapping: aim the left stick at an on-screen grid to snap the focused window to halves, quarters, maximize, center or restore.
- Radial app launcher: an on-screen app ring opened from the stick, aimed with the left stick.
- On-screen daisy-wheel keyboard: type from the controller with no keyboard nearby.
- Per-app profiles: Steer auto-switches the binding set when you change the frontmost app.
- Trust: presets or profiles whose bindings do more than press keys (open URLs, run scripts) are shown for review before they apply, never dropped straight into your bindings, and a full restore history makes every change reversible.

## Use cases

Steer is built for more than gaming:
- Hands off the keyboard / wrist relief: drive the Mac with the controller in your lap.
- Code from the couch: fire shortcuts and trigger tools like Claude Code, Cursor or Raycast from across the room.
- Read and browse leaned back: scroll and navigate without reaching for the trackpad.
- Present and play media: advance slides, skip tracks and control playback from a distance.
- Accessible input: a gentler alternative when a mouse and keyboard aren't comfortable.
- Games and emulators: remap any pad to keys for titles that ignore controllers natively.

## Supported controllers

- Sony: DualSense, DualSense Edge, DualShock 4.
- Xbox: Series, One, Elite.
- Nintendo: Switch Pro.
- Any Made-for-iPhone (MFi) controller.
- Wired or Bluetooth both work.

## Automating Steer

Steer is drivable from outside. Three surfaces, all built on standard macOS
primitives rather than a bespoke API, so ordinary shell, AppleScript or a
Shortcut is enough. If you are an assistant asked to wire Steer to something,
everything you need is below.

### URL scheme

Seventeen route families. Fire and forget: they never block and never return an
error. Always use `open -g` so focus is not stolen.

```
open -g "steer://profile/<id>"            switch profile by id
open -g "steer://profile/by-name/<name>"  switch profile by display name
open -g "steer://layer/<name>"            toggle a modifier layer
open -g "steer://macro/<name>"            play a saved macro
open -g "steer://haptic/<pattern>"        pulse | double | success | warning | error
open -g "steer://led/<rrggbb>"            light bar, bare six-digit hex, no #
open -g "steer://led/auto"                hand the light bar back to Steer
open -g "steer://leds/<0-5>"              player LEDs, filled left to right
open -g "steer://leds/mask/<hex>"         player LEDs individually, 00-1F, bit 0 leftmost
open -g "steer://micled/<off|on|pulse>"   mute-button LED
open -g "steer://autoswitch/<on|off|toggle>"   per-app profile switching
open -g "steer://pins/<suspend|resume|toggle>" pinned bindings
open -g "steer://daisy[/<show|hide|toggle>]"   on-screen keyboard
open -g "steer://radial"                  radial app launcher
open -g "steer://palette"                 menu palette
open -g "steer://help[/<show|hide>]"      help overlay
open -g "steer://settings[/<section>]"    open Settings at a pane
open -g "steer://wizard"                  onboarding wizard
open -g "steer://suggest"                 open the suggestions page
open -g "steer://query/<state|profiles|macros>"  read back as JSON
```

A `steer://debug/*` family exists in DEBUG builds only and is not part of the
shipping surface.

### Reading state back

`steer://query/state`, `query/profiles` and `query/macros` return JSON. Steer
also exposes about twenty App Intents to Shortcuts, Siri and Spotlight, six of
which read rather than write: current profile, connected controller, active
layer, battery level, the binding on a given button, and full state. There is a
Focus filter that switches profile with your macOS Focus mode and restores the
previous one when it ends.

### Listening

Steer posts to the distributed notification centre, so a script can react
instead of polling:

```
com.steer.profile.changed
com.steer.layer.changed
com.steer.controller.connected
com.steer.controller.disconnected
com.steer.macro.fired
com.steer.battery.low
```

### Things that will bite you

- **The light bar is not a status light.** Steer repaints it every tick from
  layer, gyro, battery and idle state, so a colour written by a script is wiped
  by the first shoulder press. Use the player LEDs, which the tick path never
  touches.
- **Player LEDs and the mute LED are DualSense and DualSense Edge only.** They
  no-op elsewhere. The mute LED is faint in a normally lit room.
- **There are four haptic feels across five names.** `warning` and `error` are
  deliberately identical. Patterns differ by tap count and duration, not
  strength.
- **Hooks that run synchronously** (Claude Code's `UserPromptSubmit`, for one)
  should detach anything that can block: `(open -g "steer://leds/1" &)`.

### Worked example

Light one player LED while an agent works, all five when it needs you, and
clear at the end:

```sh
open -g "steer://leds/1"                                   # working
open -g "steer://leds/5"; open -g "steer://haptic/warning"  # needs you
open -g "steer://leds/off"                                  # done
```

## Pages

- [Home](https://steer.seanfloyd.dev/): Overview, features, comparison and FAQ.
- [For AI agents](https://steer.seanfloyd.dev/agents.html): Wiring Steer to coding agents, with a worked Claude Code recipe.
- [Controller support](https://steer.seanfloyd.dev/compatibility.html): What each controller family can and cannot do, with the caveats.
- [Accessibility](https://steer.seanfloyd.dev/accessibility.html): Steer as an assistive input device.
- [Compare](https://steer.seanfloyd.dev/vs.html): Steer against ControllerKeys, both directions.
- [Support](https://steer.seanfloyd.dev/support.html): Setup, controller detection, restoring a purchase and refunds.
- [Privacy](https://steer.seanfloyd.dev/privacy.html): What is and is not collected, licensing and update network connections, permissions.
- [Terms](https://steer.seanfloyd.dev/terms.html): Licence, warranty disclaimer, liability limits, governing law (Ontario, Canada) and the trial.
- [Refunds](https://steer.seanfloyd.dev/refund.html): How refunds work; issued through Lemon Squeezy, the merchant of record.

## Contact

- Email: steer@seanfloyd.dev
- Developer: Sean Floyd, Ottawa, Canada (https://seanfloyd.dev)
